episodes.title: The Co-Founders of Claude AI Tell Oprah About the Impact Artificial Intelligence Has on Your Life
episodes.description: Apple Podcasts / Megaphone episode metadata: Anthropic co-founders Dario Amodei and Daniela Amodei sit down with Oprah for an in-depth discussion on artificial intelligence and its impact on daily life. The episode covers Anthropic as a major AI company behind Claude, ethical responsibility and moral questions in advanced AI, their model Mythos, government regulation, everyday risks and benefits, jobs, children, emotional relationships with AI, Claude and underage users, why Claude does not incentivize users, Anthropic's public benefit corporation structure, and the company's refusal of the Pentagon's request to remove guardrails from Claude. Timestamps from Apple metadata: 00:00 welcome Daniela and Dario Amodei; 01:47 Dario on human extinction; 04:40 what is the AI race; 08:00 Anthropic's safety concerns; 09:26 why they are a public benefit corporation; 11:20 how the siblings formed Anthropic; 13:00 can AI be created responsibly; 14:12 benefits and harms; 18:52 people falling in love with AI; 20:30 Claude and underage users; 22:18 how Claude does not incentivize users; 28:33 refusal to remove guardrails; 35:10 hard decisions; 38:35 regulation; 40:45 whether people should view AI negatively; 43:00 the AI problem to solve; 48:20 men use AI more than women; 49:30 what is Mythos; 53:20 what keeps them up at night; 54:15 what keeps ego in check; 58:20 will they get this right; 59:15 are others doing this responsibly; 01:03:25 meaning of a well-lived life. Audio URL is available from Apple/RSS and should be transcribed with Whisper when the runner has Whisper installed.

episodes.title: How Anthropic Uses Claude Fable 5 With Mike Krieger
episodes.description: Apple Podcasts / iTunes lookup episode metadata: Mike Krieger, Instagram cofounder and head of Anthropic Labs, discusses building with Fable 5 months before public release and how the model made his existing productivity, strategy and time-management workflows feel outdated. The episode covers how Fable reshaped Krieger's workflow, when to use Sonnet versus Fable, what a weekend-built media tracker reveals about agent-native architecture, collapsing software build cost, whether software engineering is over, how Anthropic engineering teams work, verification mechanics, what people should build with the model, and dynamic workflows. Timestamps from metadata: 0:03 Introduction; 1:48 how Fable reshaped Mike's workflow; 4:48 Sonnet versus Fable; 10:06 media tracker and agent-native architecture; 15:00 cost to build collapsed; 19:03 software engineering; 21:48 Anthropic engineering teams; 38:39 verification mechanics; 44:39 what to build; 47:24 dynamic workflows. Links mentioned include Mike Krieger on X, Anthropic Labs, Claude Code and Every. No transcript is stored in this curated entry.

episodes.title: Most of the Web Will Never Get APIs for AI Agents | Dhruv Batra
episodes.show: Chain of Thought | AI Agents, Infrastructure & Engineering
episodes.description: Apple Podcasts / iTunes lookup episode metadata: Dhruv Batra is co-founder and chief scientist of Yutori, building specialized browser and computer-use agents. He previously led embodied AI at Meta's FAIR lab, trained robots in simulation, and shipped the image question-answering model on Ray-Ban Meta glasses. The core claim is that most of the web will never expose APIs for agents: school district sites, small business pages, government offices and long-tail e-commerce are built for humans, so agents must operate through the existing human web. The episode frames this as 'pixels in, clicks out' and discusses Yutori's Navigator perceiving pixels, writing JavaScript to shorten task trajectories, running 2-3x faster and 4-5x cheaper than Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5 on browser tasks, learning from live websites, using URL query parameters as privileged verifiers, Chinese open-weight models, smart glasses / robotics perception-action loops, smaller on-device models, and why the company is called Yutori. Chapters from metadata: 00:00 Pixels in, clicks out; 01:37 why most web will never get APIs; 08:47 aggregation, specialization and human friction; 11:39 digital niches and specialized models; 16:41 web heavy tail and browser agents; 20:40 Yutori Navigator and Scouts; 24:08 N1.5 writing JavaScript; 27:45 training on live websites; 33:29 open source, FAIR and Chinese frontier; 37:22 agent frameworks: OpenClaw, Hermes, heartbeats; 40:57 non-technical users adopting agents; 44:25 smart glasses, robotics and embodied AI; 50:57 compute demand and smaller on-device models; 53:12 why Yutori. No transcript is stored in this curated entry.
